result.reasoning:
First, list the candy bags and their colors: - Gumballs: red, orange, yellow, green, blue, brown (6 colors) - Candy corn: yellow, white, orange (3 colors) - Gumdrops: red, green, purple, yellow, orange (5 colors) - Banana taffy: yellow (1 color) - Chocolate chips: brown, white (2 colors) - Gummy bears: red, green, yellow, orange (4 colors)
Define primary colors as red, yellow, blue (common pigment primaries).
First person selects "only primary colors": only banana taffy has just yellow, a primary color (others have non‑primaries). So first picks banana taffy.
Remaining: gumballs, candy corn, gumdrops, chocolate chips, gummy bears.
Second person selects "no primary colors": chocolate chips have brown and white, no red/yellow/blue. So second picks chocolate chips.
Remaining: gumballs, candy corn, gumdrops, gummy bears.
Third person selects "all the primary colors": gumballs have red, yellow, blue. So third picks gumballs.
Remaining: candy corn, gumdrops, gummy bears.
Fourth person selects "neither the most nor the least colors of the remaining bags": - Candy corn: 3 colors (least) - Gumdrops: 5 colors (most) - Gummy bears: 4 colors (neither most nor least) So fourth picks gummy bears.
Remaining: candy corn, gumdrops.
Fifth person selects "with their favorite color, green": gumdrops have green, candy corn does not. So fifth picks gumdrops.
Remaining bag after all picks: candy corn.
A second bag of the first person’s choice (banana taffy) is added to the remaining bag. So after addition, there are two bags: candy corn and banana taffy.
The two candies are banana taffy and candy corn. In alphabetical order: banana taffy, candy corn.
Casing: The candy names are given in lowercase in the user query, so preserve exact lowercase. No sentence‑casing fallback needed for this list.
